summary: Fowler argues that application boundaries are fundamentally social rather than technical constructs. He responds to Service Oriented Architecture proponents who claim applications are becoming obsolete by pointing out that the difficulty in defining application boundaries actually proves their persistence. Applications exist as social agreements between developers, business users, and budget holders, not as objectively definable technical entities. The boundaries we draw are shaped more by human relationships and politics than by functional or technical considerations.
key_points: Application boundaries are notoriously difficult to define precisely, as evidenced by debates like whether browsers are part of operating systems
SOA proponents predict applications will disappear in favor of assembled services, but Fowler disagrees
Applications are defined by three social perspectives: developer perception of code unity, business perception of functional unity, and budget allocation as a single initiative
Boundary decisions are driven primarily by human inter-relationships and politics rather than technical factors
Acknowledging the social nature of applications is necessary for clearer thinking about software architecture
money_quotes: One of the undecided problems of software development is deciding what the boundaries of a piece of software is.
We can draw application boundaries in hundred arbitrarily different ways.
It's our nature to group things together and organize groups of people around these groups.
These boundaries are drawn primarily by human inter-relationships and politics rather than technical and functional considerations.
To think about this more clearly I think we have to recognize this uncomfortable fact.
key_insight: Applications are social constructions defined by human perception and organizational politics, not objective technical boundaries.
